title: Average Sepal Length by Iris Species
interpretation: This bar chart displays the mean sepal length for each of the three iris species. Iris-virginica has the longest average sepal length at 6.588 cm, followed by Iris-versicolor at 5.936 cm, and Iris-setosa at 5.006 cm. The clear separation between species suggests sepal length is a strong discriminative feature for species classification. The 1.58 cm difference between the longest and shortest indicates substantial morphological divergence.

title: Petal Length vs Petal Width by Species
interpretation: This scatter plot reveals three distinct clusters corresponding to the three iris species. Iris-setosa forms a tight cluster in the lower-left corner with small petal dimensions (length < 2 cm, width < 0.6 cm), completely separated from the other two species. Iris-versicolor and Iris-virginica show a strong positive correlation between petal length and width, with virginica generally having larger petals. The clear separation of setosa suggests it is morphologically distinct, while versicolor and virginica show some overlap but remain largely separable.

title: Distribution of Sepal Width Across Species
interpretation: This box plot reveals that Iris-setosa has notably wider sepals with a higher median (3.4 cm) and less overlap with the other species. The interquartile range for setosa (3.2 to 3.7 cm) is positioned above most values for versicolor and virginica. Iris-versicolor has the narrowest sepals with a median of 2.8 cm, while Iris-virginica has a median of 3.0 cm. The whiskers show that setosa ranges from 3.0 to 4.4 cm, versicolor from 2.0 to 3.4 cm, and virginica from 2.2 to 3.8 cm, indicating setosa's distinctively wider sepals.

title: Correlation Heatmap of Morphometric Features
interpretation: This heatmap reveals several important correlation patterns. Petal length and petal width show an extremely strong positive correlation (0.963), indicating these dimensions scale together. Sepal length also correlates strongly with petal length (0.872) and petal width (0.818). Interestingly, sepal width shows weak negative correlation with petal dimensions (-0.428 with petal length, -0.366 with petal width) and a slight negative correlation with sepal length (-0.118). This suggests sepal width behaves differently from other measurements and may provide unique discriminative information for species classification.
